title: Yu-Shiba-Rusinov States in Ising Superconductors
authors: Michael Hein, Juan Carlos Cuevas, Wolfgang Belzig
abstract: The nature of the superconducting state in two-dimensional transition-metal dichalcogenides remains under active debate. A widely used description invokes so-called Ising superconductivity. In this work, we investigate theoretically this pairing state by employing single magnetic impurities as local probes of the superconducting condensate. We analyze the formation of Yu-Shiba-Rusinov bound states in the presence of Ising spin-orbit coupling and an in-plane magnetic field to study how their spectral properties encode the underlying pairing structure. We identify distinct features in the bound-state spectrum and tunneling response that differentiate this system from conventional superconductors. Our results demonstrate that magnetic impurities provide a sensitive probe of the structure of the superconducting state and yield experimentally accessible signatures of unconventional aspects of Ising superconductivity.
summary: This paper theoretically investigates how magnetic impurities can serve as local probes to identify the unique features of Ising superconductivity in 2D materials. It demonstrates that the broken spin-rotation symmetry leads to a split YSR spectrum and distinct transport signatures. These findings provide a roadmap for using STM to distinguish unconventional Ising pairing from standard BCS superconductivity.
main_problem: The study aims to identify experimental signatures that distinguish Ising superconductors (ISCs) from conventional BCS superconductors by investigating how Yu-Shiba-Rusinov (YSR) bound states encode the underlying pairing structure.
main_result: The researchers found that the YSR spectrum in ISCs splits into two spin-dependent branches and can host two distinct pairs of subgap states, with the spectrum's features (like avoided crossings) depending on the impurity moment direction and in-plane magnetic fields.
method: The work employs a quasi-classical Green's function formalism using the Eilenberger equation and the Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) framework, utilizing a valley-dependent unitary transformation and Schur-complement reduction.
model_or_system: The system consists of a single magnetic impurity (modeled via the Anderson model in the classical spin limit) on a 2D transition-metal dichalcogenide (1H-TMD) substrate exhibiting Ising spin-orbit coupling and an in-plane magnetic field.
key_observables: YSR bound-state energy spectrum, tunneling response (STM), local density of states (LDOS), supercurrent (current-phase relation), and the superconducting gap.
important_parameters: Ising spin-orbit coupling strength, in-plane magnetic field (Bx), exchange coupling (J), on-site potential (U0), hybridization strength (Gamma), and impurity magnetic moment orientation (theta).
main_assumptions: The impurity is treated in the classical/static spin limit (kBTK << Delta), the density of states is assumed constant in the low-energy limit, and pairing within the Gamma pocket and inter-valley couplings are neglected.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the normal state band structure and the TMD lattice; Figure 7 illustrates the superconducting gap as a function of the in-plane magnetic field; Figure 9 displays the components of the effective magnetic moment and bound state energies.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of identifying Ising superconductivity, describes the theoretical model and Hamiltonian, details the Green's function and symmetry analysis methods, presents results regarding spectral splitting and phase transitions, and concludes with experimental implications.
why_interesting: The paper explores quantum phase transitions and the breaking of spin-rotation symmetry in a many-body system, which is relevant to understanding how local impurities can be used to probe and manipulate the topological or unconventional properties of a many-body condensate.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
